Khalid : Master Maryānus, I have heard that alchemy is not merely about mystical transformation, but also about tools and substances—about the craft itself. What do these instruments and materials reveal about the practice? Maryānus : Indeed, Khalid. Alchemy requires both the philosopher’s insight and the craftsman’s hand. Let us begin with the tools . The alembic , for example, is a vessel designed for distillation . By heating substances and collecting their vapors, we purify and separate their essences. Khalid : A tool of separation, then. And is this alembic an invention of our age? Maryānus : No, it was perfected by our predecessors. Maria the Jewess devised its earliest form, but Arabic alchemists refined it into an essential apparatus, indispensable for creating elixirs and purifying mercury. Khalid : And what of the athanor ? Maryānus : Ah, the athanor , or the “furnace of transformation.” It maintains steady heat for long experiments, symbolizing patience. Without it, processes like calcination —breaking down metals to remove impurities—would falter. Khalid : But tell me, Master, of the materials themselves. What are these substances the alchemist commands? Maryānus : We work with the elements of nature —sulfur, mercury, and salt. Sulfur is the flame, mercury the fluid metal, and salt the preserver. Together, they represent the essence of all matter. Khalid : And how are they refined? Maryānus : Take sulfuric acid , the “oil of vitriol.” It is drawn from green vitriol by heating it in a vessel. This acid, Khalid, dissolves metals, separating the pure from the impure. Khalid : Such power from humble origins! What of more complex preparations, like aqua regia ? Maryānus : A mixture of nitric acid and hydrochloric acid , capable of dissolving even gold, the king of metals. It is the pinnacle of dissolution, Khalid, and a symbol of ultimate purification. Khalid : These acids and their making—are they not dangerous? Maryānus : They are tools of great potency, and so they demand respect. But they also mark the alchemist’s skill in transforming raw substances into agents of perfection. Khalid : Master, are these techniques solely for alchemy? Maryānus : No, Khalid. Arabic physicians like al-Rāzī use them to refine medicines. The techniques you learn in the laboratory can heal the body as well as the metal. Khalid : A true unity of science and art, then. And these methods—they reached beyond us, did they not? Maryānus : Yes, Khalid. In the Latin West, our techniques crossed the seas and mountains. The translators preserved the distillation of thought , even as they infused it with their own beliefs. Khalid : So, alchemy, Master, is not only the work of the individual but the work of many hands, across time and space. Maryānus : Precisely, Khalid. From alembic to athanor, from sulfur to aqua regia, alchemy stands as a bridge—between craft and philosophy, between cultures, and between the material and the divine.
